#Denali2019 #TheColdOne #timetoreflect
Just wow! It's a superb mountain for improving your mountaineering / survival skills! #nobearsthough 🙂 or you could climb the mountain instead of crossfit 😛 when you are putting together a plan at home, 1km of vertical climb per day, 45kg of gear to carry, 5 camps, 20 days, etc are just numbers and you don't "feel" them. When you get to the mountain, the reality hits haaard! #maaaan. In short, each day was tougher and tougher.. and we pushed harder and harder.. until our bodies could not take it any more.
No summit this time. In my view, the reasons are fairly simple and I'll just note these down for the next time:
1. Food. Anything works until Camp 4. Bring regular food even if it weights a bit more. Bring more Pringles. Eat! Even if you don't like it. Eat!
2. Rest. Even if you can push harder, don't. Take a break. Eat a chocolate. Drink some tea. Relax. 
3. Train. Make sure to run at least 3 half-marathons in a row. It's much harder up there!
4. Tent. Check the winds your tent could sustain. You can possibly wait out the storm in Camp 5.
5. Larger group. Invite more friends to join 🙂 improves morale and helps to spread the weight.
#longpost 
But let's start at the beginning.. 
We planned to spend 2 days in Talkeetna (a gateway town to Denali). However, there weren't many climbers and we got a chance to fly a day earlier. Screw that prep we planned to do beforehand! We'll just learn on the go! 🙂 #letsflyout #ToKahiltnaBaseCamp! 
Days 1-3. Base Camp - Camp 3 (2200m - 3400m).
• A small chopper plane dropped us off to a glacier and now it's only me and my teammate against the mountain. With each step you get further and further away from civilization. It's a distressing thought - the further you walk, the harder it will be to get back... and if sth happens the weather conditions need to be good enough for the plane to fly in.. #norescueinbadweather. Walking itself is also a risky activity - you walk over #crevasses all the time. You don't see them, but if ice breaks, you may fall into a deep hole. Hopefully, you don't drag your teammate with you inside and he has the energy to set up anchors and pull you out 🙂 20kg in the backpack and 25kg on the sledge are a really good way of getting rid of this energy. While there were around 300 people on the mountain at the time, they were mostly in Camp 3 and Camp 4 acclimatizing. Thus for 20+ km you are pretty much on your own. If the sun is shining, it is all good! The views are great and you can just follow someone else's footsteps. It becomes lonelier when winds pick up and snow showers try to blind you. The heart really starts racing faster when you see only #white wherever you look. It is a strange feeling - as if you were blindfolded but everything is just white 🙂 no sense of objects or mountains nearby and I was happy if I could at least see Aurimas nearby (we were tied to the same rope). #randomwalking. 
• To sum up Day 2, you don't know the terrain, you can't see anything/anyone, it's extremely windy and cold, you are in a knee-deep snow, sweating and cursing, the ground below you might break up any time and you still have to carry this 45kg of gear into the abbyss. I know, you must be getting jealous already! #amazingtimes! 🙂
• Oh, and when you are tired after 10h walking uphill and decide to camp, you need to build a shelter (i.e. dig a hole, set up a tent and build a wall around it). #walkingdead #exhaustionatitsbest. Then you also have to spend a few hours melting snow and cooking. Note: food does not get any tastier if you add a bit of fuel to it 😛 #JamieOliver. Going to sleep, hoping that our stomachs can handle it.
Days 4-5. Camp 3 to Camp 4. (3400m - 4300m)
• The mood changes when you reach Camp 3 which is fairly crowded (~100people). #chatting #snoring #caching. Quite many climbers go up and down, so the trail is well wanded and you can follow one or another group. Though, the struggle now is the icy part of the route (3800m) which seems around 45 degrees steep. Sledges suddenly become alive and start to behave like little kids - they slide, roll and do whetever it takes to annoy you and make you miserable. You have crampons on and ice axe in your hand. Though, one bigger mistake (or a simple slip) and off you go off a cliff with all your treasured stuff. And you'd probably drag your friend down with you.. #riskybusiness
• Then there's the #WindyCorner #4100m where wind tries to again blow you off or at least make you slip on ice and break some bones. The sledges continue rolling, falling, annoying.. other climbers are annoyed that u're too slow and the path is too narrow to let someone pass by. And my sledges just keep rolling.. lost some wands and a fuel bottle already. #keepstuffinsidethebag. We'll have to risk going back, descending and collecting the fuel bottle (the wands were too far down and not important enough). Enough is enough, I use the rest of my spare energy to lift the 25kg sledge with one hand, take an ice axe into the other hand and carefully traverse the narrow path. The #icy part is over and now we're back in business with crevasses. We had to again cross over several of them. #notsuredeepornot. Slim ice bridges above the crevasses don't seem very reliable - you have this feeling that it's a 50% chance that either you or your partner would fall into one 🙂 you've been on your feet suffering for 7h already and your eyes are starting to look crossways. One false hill after another until you reach Camp 4. Honestly, 1km of vertical climb with 45kg of gear (single carry) is idiotic! But I'd do it again because climbing the same terrain twice feels retarded as well 🙂 #WouldNotFindMotivation #SufferOnce!
• There were ~200 people in Camp 4. Everyone's waiting for a good weather window. Some have been staying here for up to 2 weeks already! This is just pure sado-mazo at its best.. we ain't gonna last that long. Aurimas has stomach problems and hasn't eaten anything proper for days. #ChocoDiet. Plus everyone at Camp 4 is talking only about.. the weather! Just like in CH! 🙂 who could survive it for too long? 😛 #weatherisoverrated #hateweathertopics! 
• Morning views are usually the best. Though, it is extremely hard to force oneself out of the warm and cozy sleeping bag. Once you do get outside, it is really worthwhile. Tents are covered with frost, there's almost no wind and the sun is about to rise.. #loveit! 
• In the afternoon the weather changes. We do a short acclimatization hike (300m vertical) until we can barely see our camp, drink some tea and go back to tents. Water. Food. Sleep. Repeat. Let's see what tomorrow brings! The forecasts are so unpredictable!
Days 6-7. Camp 4 to Camp 5 and summit attempt. (4300m - 5200m - 5300m - 4300m)
• Alarm wakes us up at 6am. The forecast has slightly improved - there's a short window for the summit bid. It is only 1.5 days which is on the borderline for us (3 days or more would be ideal). We stay put until 10am - no changes. Next potential window is in around one week's time.. possibly, too late for us as Aurimas' stomach still can't handle the food. And it honestly sucks spending time up that high. #notmycomfortzoneeither. Waiting out the storm would considerably weaken us down.
• Looks like we have to go to Camp 5 #NOW or forget it! We are behind the schedule to start as it will take around 10h for us to get there. We pack only necessary gear and food/gas for 3 days. #SingleCarryAgain! #OnlyBackpack. Still weights too much! #maaan, this is not going to work! It feels like the backpack will break my spine before we reach Camp 5! #miseryatitsbest #lowmorale. We leave Camp 4 anyway at mid-day 12:00. Some 95% of the other climbers remain in Camp 4 waiting for a longer window or just go to make a cache (bring half of the stuff up and hide it in a hole) at 4900m. #shouldhavestartedearlier #latedecision #sh*t #maybewecanstilldoit #letsjusttry.
• Small hill. #NeverEnds. Step by step. No rush. Everyone knows the drill. Takes time to reach fixed ropes. #FinallyThere. We must have done 400m vertical climb. #4700m. #ughugh. Fixed ropes part is easy for me. We secure ourselves and use ascenders. It is mostly hands that I use to pull myself up. I don't want to be a #bottleneck, but luckily other people above me are slower. I get plenty of time to rest on the ropes. #Done. We are at around #4900m. Just a bit more. We sit down to rest and I take off my backpack. The sheer weight of it freaks me out.. how the hell will I put so much weight back on my shoulders?? #Damn we feel tired.. some tea. I check the time - we are not as slow as expected! The view is superb, I convince myself to take some pics. That cheers me up a bit! 🙂 We have to get going. Shouldn't be too far. We hope that the peak of the next hill is the end of our suffering. But it is a false peak. #crap. We sit down. Misery is all around us. We're contemplating about turning around. Aurimas is extremely tired and I feel like my body could just break any minute. #Iaintgivingup! Aurimas suggests that he won't have the energy to attempt the summit tomorrow. I still hope that he will or that I could find some other team to join in. #Icanstilldoit! We agree to continue a bit more as Camp 5 seems to be right after this next hill. However, it is another false peak.. And another one.. and another one.. 😕 I can barely walk and the altmeter shows that we still need to do another 100m of vertical ascent. We secure ourselves to all anchors we can find. #safetyfirst. This slows us down considerably, but we are not taking any chances. At least not at this level of tiredness. Finally, after 9h 30min of extremely grueling walk two zombies reach Camp 5. #5200m. #canbarelystand. #waterplease #wearedone. There are fewer than 10 tents around. We need to set up ours. Dig some snow, build protections, etc. Takes roughly 2 hours. It is nearly midnight and I am still melting water for the night. It is getting colder and colder outside. It is already 1am and I still don't have enough water. #trouble. 2 liters will suffice. I know that I have to go and sleep at least a few hours. I set the alarm at 4am to start boiling water again for the summit attempt.. I don't know where my body will find the energy, but my will says that I have to try!
• Alarm rings. I don't feel like I've slept at all. Aurimas does not even move. I start melting snow and checking forecasts. No change. Looks like I have to turn around at 13:00 in order to make it to Camp 4 before the storm hits. #tooshort #butmaybejustmaybe. We can't stay in Camp 5 as it is not well protected from wind - as per forecast, the wind should reach 100km/h. And it should stay as such for 3 days. Hence, we would be stuck in the tent. Of course, if the tent survives the wind.. #canit? I talk to our neighbours - they are 3 Czech guys that we met a few days ago. 1 guy is willing to join me for the attempt, 2 others are completely exhausted. We agree with Aurimas that he would wait for me to get back and we'd go down together. We rope up with the Czech guy and leave at around 7am. I really struggle to keep the pace and continue walking like a zombie. Even though I carry only essentials, the backpack feels too heavy 😕 I start to feel that my motivation begins to abandon me. I check my watch - way behind the schedule.. #ohno. Looks like I'll have to make a call sooner or later. #ohnoohnoohno.. heh.. ok, mr rational Paulius, think it through. The fact is that you will not reach the summit in time; however, your Czech partner may decide to abandon you and risk summiting on his own. That would put your and Aurimas' health in jeopardy. Plus you are wasting the energy which you'll need for getting down. Remember the full weight of the backpack? Yes, you'll have to take it down! On your own shoulders! #hatebeingright. I check that we are now at 5300m. How about just a little bit more..? #Wait. What difference does it make if it is 5300m or 5500m if you still haven't reached the top? #None #Zero #Nada. The miracle will not happen and you will not get any energy boosts! This is not a game on Xbox - stop dreaming! You should have trained beforehand for this! #alright. I wipe away a tear. #Iamdone #DenaliWins. #NextTime. And I as turn around to tell the Czech climber that I give up, I slip and slide down some 20m. I managed to stop the fall with the ice axe. #whew. Now I am done for real! (No, it is not a 'sign for turning around'. It is just me being clumsy and tired). Czech climber decides to continue the summit attempt on his own and we part ways.. #reallyhard. I don't feel liberated; I feel beaten.. broken.. how much I'd want to continue..
• At least Aurimas is happy to see me 🙂 that makes his decisions easier. The sun is shining. I go to sleep for a few hours and we'll go down to Camp 4. My mind is still eager to leave a cache and try again in a week's time. My body (as well as Aurimas) advices that it would be borderline stupid. #damn.. #thisisit.
• The storm catches us on the way down. It started to get worse at around 3pm. Hence, even if I turned around at 1pm, we wouldn't have had the time to get to Camp 4 before it hit. The weather window was shorter than expected. We are being hit hard by snow showers and can't see much in front of us. It slows us down a lot. Though, we are not too worried - we've invested quite a bit into gear and we don't really feel cold. Worst case, we could probably just camp anywhere on the way. It took us forever (i.e. 5h 30min) to get to Camp 4 and set up a tent. #finallyhere. 
Days 8-9. Camp 4 - Base Camp. (4300m - 2200m)
• We've spent the next 36h in the tent. Physically and morally broken. We would just get out to melt some snow and undig our tent once it got nearly covered by snow. Not that you can do more during the storm. We just had to wait it out. My heart said that we have around 12 more days to rest, recover and reset before the next good weather window; my mind and body would remind me of the cruel hike up we've done a few days ago. Whereas Aurimas in his thoughts was already fishing King Salmon in Alaska.. 🙂 Looks like another summit attempt will have to wait for a few years.. (Why so long? I am planning marry my #suzadetine next year 😘 ). We gave away the food to fellow climbers, packed remaining gear and in around 11 grueling hours we were back at Base Camp. #Finito #TillNextTime. 
• The Czechs? We haven't met them again. They did not descend to Camp 4 before we left. When we left Camp 5 it was only theirs and rangers' tents still standing. The 2 guys decided to wait for their colleague to come back. They were reinforcing snow walls and expecting to wait out the storm there as they suggested that their colleague would not come back without the summit.. they seemed fairly experienced and we hope that everything worked out!
• Hey ho! #AlaskaRoadtrip begins! 🙂
-------
P.S.:
1) Guiding. Only around 30% of climbers used official guiding companies. Others either went on their own or had illegal climbing guides.
2) Solo. We met around 10 people going solo. There probably were more. In my view, going solo does not add that much additional risk as they tend to follow other climbing groups. Of course, one just needs to be extra careful and strong enough to keep the pace of the leading group.
3) Following others. There aren't that many people commuting between Base Camp and Camp 3. Also, if you prefer climbing with others around, you should not leave later in the day as most groups start their climbs between 8-10am.
4) Rushing to the top. We met teams in Camp 4 who have already spent 2 weeks there. We have also met people who summited and returned to Anchorage within 12 days. Hence, if the weather allows it is up to you and your physical abilities.
5) Weather. From Base Camp until Camp 4 it does not really matter. If you take recommended gear, you should be able to easily persevere any weather at this altitude.
6) Camping. The same. If you take recommended gear, you should be able to camp anywhere you like between Base Camp and Camp 4. Got tired? Build a tent next to the road and rest.
7) Single vs double carry. Again, different tactics but up to you. I'd do more sports and single carry up until Camp 4. Then cache at 4900m.
-----
Last but not least, our huge THANK YOU! goes to:
Andrius Kirdeikis for all the advice on the route, gear as well as moral support. It felt as if we had climbed the mountain before! 🙂
Lukla.lt - patogiai kelionei for all the gear and Tadas Jeršovas for all the tips. There was one thing we felt certain of and did not worry about at all - our gear. Imagine hanging there at 4800m on fixed ropes - your rope got entangled, the storm started and wind/snow is hitting you with full force. You don't panic and take your time to reset because you don't feel cold at all and just trust the gear.
Julius Survila for teaching us how to rescue each other in case we fell into a crevasse. Luckily, we did not have to use these skills in practice 🙂 #maybenexttime 😛
Karolis and Fredrik for being amazing weather boys! You should consider changing jobs 🙂
Lastly, to our biggest fans Jorune and Kristina for letting boys out to play in the wilderness! 😘
